scenario.title: The Insurance Rule Engine
scenario.narrative: A premium auto-insurance algorithm approved a high-risk policy by mistake. The logic is simply: `is_vip AND has_good_record`. The QA team insists their tests were thorough enough.

claim: We achieved total coverage of the rules. We tested when VIP was True and False. We tested when Good Record was True and False. Every single condition was flipped! It's exhaustive.
charges: The defense claims they've achieved 'total coverage' because every individual condition was observed as both True and False in their test set.
As Prosecutor, point out the critical flaw in their reasoning regarding combination coverage.
fragments: You achieved Branch Condition Combination coverage,
but you completely ignored the combinations.
but you completely ignored the independent pairs.
is_vip is True, but has_good_record is False?
is_vip is False, but has_good_record is True?
is_vip is False, and has_good_record is False?
That untested combination is where the bug hides.
dialogue_valid_sequences: but you completely ignored the combinations.
is_vip is True, but has_good_record is False?
That untested combination is where the bug hides.
but you completely ignored the combinations.
is_vip is False, but has_good_record is True?
That untested combination is where the bug hides.
dialogue_correct_explanations: Exactly. Flipping each condition individually only satisfies Branch Condition (BC) coverage. To be truly exhaustive for 2 conditions, you need all 4 combinations (BCC). Missing the T-F and F-T combinations left a massive vulnerability.

Exactly. Flipping each condition individually only satisfies Branch Condition (BC) coverage. To be truly exhaustive for 2 conditions, you need all 4 combinations (BCC). Missing the T-F and F-T combinations left a massive vulnerability.
wrong_answer_explanation: That objection doesn't dismantle their specific claim. They did test each condition as True and False. But what happens when the conditions interact in ways they didn't test?
hints: They tested (True, True) and (False, False). This means VIP was seen as T and F, and Record was seen as T and F.
This satisfies Branch Condition coverage. But what combinations did they miss entirely?
Look for the missing combinations like (True, False) or (False, True).
